source_alignment.subject: Science
source_alignment.source_statement: Associate the brightness of a lamp or the volume of a buzzer with the number and voltage of cells used in the circuit; compare and give reasons for variations in how components function, including the brightness of bulbs, the loudness of buzzers and the on/off position of switches; use recognised symbols when representing a simple circuit in a diagram.
source_alignment.derived_objective_note: This pack extends Year 4 complete-circuit reasoning into safe Year 6 circuit modelling: learners vary cells and components, read evidence, explain outcomes, and translate between pictorial circuits and recognised circuit symbols.

objective.child_goal: I can explain and model how changing a circuit changes its components.
objective.statement: Use safe circuit models, evidence and recognised symbols to explain how cells, switches, bulbs and buzzers work in simple series circuits.
misconceptions: Thinks adding more cells is always safe and always makes every component work better.
Thinks electricity is used up by the first component in a series circuit.
Thinks a switch changes the strength of electricity rather than opening or closing the path.
Confuses a circuit diagram symbol with the real object's appearance.
Thinks a dim bulb must always mean a broken bulb rather than a circuit variable to investigate.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A measure linked to how strongly a cell can push charges around a circuit.
vocabulary.audio_script: Voltage helps us compare cells in a circuit model. More cells can make some components brighter or louder, if the components are suitable.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Cell cards marked one point five volts connected in series.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A circuit where components are connected in one loop.
vocabulary.audio_script: In a series circuit, the components are connected in a single complete loop.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: One continuous loop from one cell terminal through two components and back.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A simple agreed drawing used to represent a circuit component.
vocabulary.audio_script: Circuit symbols help scientists and engineers draw circuits clearly.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A real bulb beside its circle-with-cross symbol.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A part of a circuit, such as a cell, bulb, buzzer, motor or switch.
vocabulary.audio_script: A component is one part of a circuit with a job to do.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A labelled component tray with cell, bulb, buzzer and switch icons.

vocabulary.child_explanation: Something you change, measure or keep the same during an investigation.
vocabulary.audio_script: A fair circuit investigation changes one variable at a time.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Three cards: change, measure, keep the same.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Trace the complete path around the circuit before we change anything.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Reactivate Year 4 complete-circuit reasoning and set a safe simulator-only context.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: We investigate circuits using safe models. A real mains socket is never part of these activities.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A low-voltage classroom-style circuit board with a clear complete loop.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Add one suitable cell at a time and observe the bulb brightness.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Introduce the link between number/voltage of cells and component output without implying unlimited safety.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: When we add suitable cells in series, the bulb may become brighter. The component must be rated for the circuit.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Cell cards snap into a series loop while a brightness meter changes from dim to brighter.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Keep the bulb and wires the same. Change only the number of cells.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Model fair testing and controlled variables in a circuit investigation.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: To explain the result, change one thing, measure one thing, and keep the rest the same.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Investigation board with change: cells; measure: brightness; keep same: bulb, wires, switch.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Match each real component to its recognised circuit symbol, then build the diagram.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Translate between concrete circuit layouts and standard symbolic representations.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A circuit diagram does not look like the real objects. It uses agreed symbols to show the connections clearly.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Split screen: pictorial circuit on the left, symbol diagram grid on the right.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Compare the bulb and buzzer when the switch is open and closed.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Explain variation in component function from path completeness and component role.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: The switch opens or closes the path. When the path is closed, suitable components can work.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Bulb glow meter and buzzer volume meter beside an open/closed switch.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Use the results table to explain why circuit B has the brighter bulb.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Check evidence-based explanation rather than guesswork.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Use the table and the circuit model together. The best answer names the changed variable and the observed effect.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Two circuit diagrams with a small results table for cells, bulb brightness and switch state.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Teach Nova why we do not test unknown batteries, plugs or mains electricity.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Repair unsafe overgeneralisation and reinforce classroom safety boundaries.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Could I try this with any battery or a wall socket? Explain the safe science rule.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Companion points to safe simulator, school cell, unknown battery and mains plug; unsafe options are locked.

manipulatives.purpose: Allow learners to vary cell number, switch state and component type in simple series circuits, then use observations to explain brightness, loudness and on/off outcomes.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners drag rated cells and components onto a snap-grid, set one variable to change, run a safe test, read output meters, and complete an explanation stem.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Brightness and loudness are represented by labelled meters and pattern changes, not colour or sound alone. Sound is muted by default with captions and haptic-free visual equivalents.

manipulatives.purpose: Help learners translate pictorial series circuits into recognised symbol diagrams.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners place circuit symbols on a grid, connect them into one loop, and compare against the pictorial circuit.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Symbols have text labels, large hit areas, keyboard ordering, audio descriptions and high-contrast mode.

manipulatives.purpose: Support evidence-led explanations using simple data on cell number, voltage, switch state, bulb brightness and buzzer volume.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners highlight the changed variable, select relevant result cells, and build a because/therefore explanation.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Tables can be read row-by-row, simplified to two rows, or converted to sentence cards for learners who find grids hard to scan.

practice.interleaving_notes: Interleave complete-loop retrieval, variable-control decisions, symbol translation and evidence explanations. Do not move to multi-component explanations until learners can name the changed variable and the observed component output.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Secure recognition of standard symbols for cells, bulbs, buzzers, motors, switches and wires.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: pictorial component + symbol choices + labelled diagram placement
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Use recognised school-level circuit symbols and avoid decorative icons that could be mistaken for symbols.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Investigate how changing the number and voltage of suitable cells can affect bulb brightness.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: same bulb + same loop + one to three rated cells + brightness meter + safety gate
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Every item must state or imply suitable low-voltage cells and must reject mains electricity or unknown power sources.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Explain bulb, buzzer and motor output using open and closed switch states.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: same circuit + switch open/closed + output prediction + reason choice
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Keep the explanation focused on path completeness, not electricity strength.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Use observations and simple tables to explain variations in brightness and loudness.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: two or three circuits + results table + highlight relevant evidence + because/therefore sentence
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Tables should stay small, with no more than four columns in core items.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Build recognised circuit diagrams from pictorial series circuits.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: pictorial circuit + symbol tray + connection grid + one-loop validation
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Accept alternative neat layouts when the same components and connections are represented.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Diagnose whether a component output is caused by an open switch, incomplete circuit, fewer cells or unsuitable component setup.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: faulty or changed circuit + observation + choose most evidence-based explanation
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Avoid unsafe repair instructions; all fixes occur inside the simulator.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Maintain vocabulary, safety rules, symbols and variable reasoning through spaced review.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: short mixed prompt alternating symbol, safety, switch, brightness and evidence items
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Retrieval items should remain low-clutter and suitable for quick classroom starters.

body.prompt: Circuit A has one 1.5 V cell. Circuit B has two matching 1.5 V cells in series. The bulb and wires are the same. Which result is most likely in the safe simulator?
choices: Circuit B's bulb is brighter
Circuit A's bulb is brighter
Both bulbs must be off
The switch changes the voltage

expected_answer.value: Circuit B's bulb is brighter

hints: Only the number of suitable cells has changed.
Compare the voltage supplied to the same bulb.
question_variants.explanation: With the same suitable bulb and a closed complete circuit, adding a matching cell in series increases the supplied voltage, so the bulb is likely to be brighter in the simulator.

body.prompt: A circuit has two cells, a buzzer and a switch. The buzzer is silent when the switch is open. What is the best reason?
choices: The open switch makes a gap, so the circuit is incomplete
The buzzer has used up all the electricity
The cells are too far away to work
The switch makes electricity weaker but still complete

expected_answer.value: The open switch makes a gap, so the circuit is incomplete

hints: Trace the path around the loop.
Can the path cross an open switch?
question_variants.explanation: An open switch creates a gap in the circuit. The buzzer works only when the path is complete and the components are suitable.

body.prompt: Choose the recognised symbol for a cell in a circuit diagram.
choices: One long line and one short line
A circle with a cross inside
A zigzag sound wave
A drawing of a real battery label
body.diagram_task: Place the cell symbol in the gap between two wires.

expected_answer.value: One long line and one short line

hints: Circuit symbols are simple agreed drawings.
A bulb is usually shown as a circle with a cross.
question_variants.explanation: A cell is represented by one long line and one short line. The symbol shows the component clearly without drawing the real object.

body.prompt: Use the table to choose the best explanation.
choices: Circuit B is brighter because it has more matching cells while the switch state is the same
Circuit B is brighter because its switch is open
Circuit A is dim because the bulb must be broken
The table does not give any evidence

expected_answer.value: Circuit B is brighter because it has more matching cells while the switch state is the same

hints: Look for what changed between A and B.
Both switches are closed, so compare the cells.
question_variants.explanation: The table shows the same switch state but a different number of matching cells. That evidence supports the explanation that the extra cell is linked to the brighter bulb.

body.prompt: Nova wants to test whether a bulb gets brighter using an unknown battery pack from home. What should they do?
choices: Do not test it; use the safe simulator or teacher-approved low-voltage equipment
Try it quickly if the bulb looks small
Connect it to a mains plug to compare
Hold the wires by hand to check if it works

expected_answer.value: Do not test it; use the safe simulator or teacher-approved low-voltage equipment

hints: Unknown power sources can be unsafe.
In this pack, all investigations happen in the simulator or with teacher-approved low-voltage equipment.
question_variants.explanation: Circuit investigations must be safe. Learners should not test unknown batteries, mains electricity or loose wires; they should use the simulator or equipment approved by an adult teacher.

body.prompt: Symbol scanner 1 at the observation dome: choose the recognised representation for cell.
body.diagram_task: Match the labelled component to its symbol.
choices: a circle containing M
an open switch gap
one long line beside one short parallel line
a circle with a cross

multimodal_routes.visual: labelled colour-independent circuit diagram and output meter
multimodal_routes.text: ordered component-and-connection list with state and observation
multimodal_routes.audio_or_adult_read: neutral prompt, circuit state and table reading without revealing the answer
multimodal_routes.tactile_or_manipulative: teacher-managed unpowered symbol cards or loop board; no learner handling of powered unknown equipment
multimodal_routes.reduced_load: one variable, one loop and one evidence row visible at a time

mission.reward: restore one functional station module and add one evidence patch to the crew log
mission.evidence_to_unlock: name the changed variable or circuit clue and justify the conclusion
mission.retry_message: The circuit has given the crew a useful clue. Freeze the model, inspect one variable, and try another evidence route.

expected_answer.value: one long line beside one short parallel line

hints: Use agreed circuit marks, not the real object's colour or appearance.
Check the mission symbol key. The unequal parallel lines represent one cell.
question_variants.explanation: The unequal parallel lines represent one cell. Circuit diagrams communicate which components are present and how they connect; decorative appearance and exact page position do not replace recognised symbols.

feedback.correct: Mission evidence secured. The unequal parallel lines represent one cell. Circuit diagrams communicate which components are present and how they connect; decorative appearance and exact page position do not replace recognised symbols.
feedback.investigate_again: Compare the pictorial component, the labelled symbol key and the text-only component list before rebuilding the same connections.
feedback.misconception_repair: That option may match 'symbol_looks_like_object'. Check the mission symbol key. The unequal parallel lines represent one cell.
feedback.safety_reminder: Use only the simulator or teacher-approved low-voltage equipment. Never use mains electricity, unknown power sources or learner-led real-circuit repairs.

body.prompt: Control-deck mission 1: a safe series circuit has a suitable lamp and the switch is open. The observed output is off. Which explanation fits?
choices: The switch uses up the electricity.
The lamp is off because cells only work near switches.
An open switch makes the voltage stronger.
The open switch leaves a gap, so the lamp is off.

body.prompt: Fault-finder mission 12: the simulator clue reads 'same lamp is dim with one cell and brighter with two suitable cells'. Which diagnosis or next simulator action uses the evidence safely?
body.observation: same lamp is dim with one cell and brighter with two suitable cells
choices: A dim lamp is always broken.
The switch must be open in both circuits.
Use a wall socket to test brightness.
The dim output is explained by the lower cell number in this matched comparison, not proof of a broken lamp.

body.prompt: Investigation planner 1: How does the number of matching cells affect lamp brightness? Which plan is fair, measurable and electrically safe?
body.question: How does the number of matching cells affect lamp brightness?
body.keep_same: same rated lamp, wires, switch state and simulator
choices: Change number of approved matching cells and several other components together, then guess lamp brightness.
Use a different circuit each time without recording which variables changed.
Include an unknown battery or mains electricity to widen the range.
Change only cell number, measure brightness, and keep the lamp, wires and closed switch the same.

hints: Change: number of approved matching cells. Measure: lamp brightness.
Keep the comparison fair by holding constant: same rated lamp, wires, switch state and simulator.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks adding more cells is always safe and always makes every component work better.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner selects unlimited cells or chooses unknown/mains power as a valid test.
repair_sequence: Lock unsafe power choices and label them as not for learner testing.
Compare one, two and three teacher-approved low-voltage cells with a rated bulb in the simulator.
Ask learner to explain both the science effect and the safety boundary.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner predicts brighter output only for suitable safe cells and rejects unknown or mains power sources.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks electricity is used up by the first component in a series circuit.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner says a second component will not work because the first bulb used all the electricity.
repair_sequence: Trace the complete loop through every component and back to the cell.
Show output meters for components in the same series loop.
Prompt a cause-effect explanation about the complete path rather than electricity being used up.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner explains that components work when they are part of a complete suitable circuit.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks a switch changes the strength of electricity rather than opening or closing the path.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner explains a dim or silent component by saying the switch is making electricity weaker.
repair_sequence: Freeze the same circuit with the switch closed and trace the complete path.
Open the switch and highlight the gap.
Ask learner to predict output from open and closed switch states before testing.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner states that a switch controls whether the path is complete.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Confuses a circuit diagram symbol with the real object's appearance.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner chooses realistic drawings instead of recognised symbols in a diagram task.
repair_sequence: Place real component images beside their standard symbols.
Morph the pictorial circuit into a symbol diagram while keeping connections highlighted.
Ask learner to rebuild the same circuit using only symbols.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner correctly matches and places symbols for cells, bulbs, buzzers and switches.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks a dim bulb must always mean a broken bulb rather than a circuit variable to investigate.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner chooses 'broken bulb' without using evidence from cell number, switch state or circuit completeness.
repair_sequence: Show a results table where the same bulb is dim with one cell and brighter with two suitable cells.
Highlight the changed variable and unchanged component.
Ask learner to write a because sentence using the evidence.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner uses table evidence to distinguish a changed variable from a broken component.

adaptive_support.low_sensory: Keep the lab visually calm; avoid sparks, shocks, alarms, flashing bulbs or sudden buzzer sounds.
adaptive_support.reduced_motion: Replace animated current traces and symbol morphs with static numbered path panels and step buttons.
adaptive_support.audio_first: Narrate circuit state, changed variable, meter readings and symbol names, with replay for every prompt.
adaptive_support.reading_support: Use short explanation stems, labelled icons and because/therefore sentence frames before open writing.
adaptive_support.attention_support: Limit the tray to task-relevant cells and components; reveal distractors only in secure and stretch tasks.
adaptive_support.processing_support: Allow learners to test one variable at a time, freeze the circuit, and inspect table rows with row highlighting.
adaptive_support.confidence_support: Frame errors as debugging evidence: 'The circuit is giving us a clue' rather than 'wrong'.
adaptive_support.eal_support: Preteach voltage, cell, series, symbol, component, switch, brighter, louder, dim, silent, safe and variable.
adaptive_support.motor_support: Offer keyboard-accessible symbol placement, snap-to-grid components, undo, and no timed drag requirement.
adaptive_support.colour_vision_support: Do not rely on red/green or brightness alone; pair output changes with labels, meters and pattern fills.

evidence.mastery_rule: Expected requires predicting and explaining component outputs in simple series circuits, using recognised symbols and safe low-voltage assumptions. Secure requires controlling variables, using table evidence, and rejecting unsafe or unsupported explanations.
evidence.parent_summary: Can use safe circuit models and diagrams to explain why bulbs change brightness, buzzers change loudness and switches turn components on or off.
evidence.teacher_evidence: Uses recognised circuit symbols, identifies changed variables, links cell number/voltage to suitable component output, and explains switch effects from circuit completeness.
next_actions: Interleave with fair-test planning and results-table interpretation.
Add retrieval that asks learners to translate between real circuits and symbol diagrams.
Move to secondary-ready language about current and potential difference only after Year 6 component-output reasoning is secure.
Use teacher-approved practical work only where school risk assessment and equipment ratings allow it.

qa.notes: Cross-year review bank reaches the 260-item pilot target with five preserved curated variants and 255 deterministic candidates covering circuit symbols and diagrams, component effects, voltage and cell reasoning, series circuits, diagnosis, fair investigations, misconceptions and electrical safety. Every generated investigation is simulator-led, rejects mains and unknown power sources, and includes SEND multimodal routes, supported non-drag interactions, rich evidence feedback and mission-based progress without speed or loss pressure. Independent science, teacher, accessibility, safeguarding, local symbol-convention and renderer review remains required before promotion.
